[PDF_PAGE 114 | score=14] Poison Gases are rare, expensive, and highly dangerous. A typical dose is a clay x glass jar weighing 5 pounds. If the jar is shattered or opened, gas fills a 10' x 10' x 10' cube. It disperses after one round un- less contained on all sides. The gas lasts :or 2d6 turns in an enclosed room; 2d6 rounds in a room with at least one open door, window, arrow slit, etc. It might be possible for creative play- :rs to create larger doses of poison gas, >ut such lethal devices are left to the DMs discretion. Poison gases generally have the ef- 'ects of poison type D, taking effect af- :er one or two minutes, inflicting 30 9oints of damage (2d6 with a successful ;ave). The damage is inflicted each tur
[PDF_PAGE 27 | score=8] Thief Kits I let alone prosper in his ways. Adventurers may be part of a thieves’ guild for easy access to equip- ment and training. They tend to be in- dependent, however, and dislike guilds that have demands beyond a simple membership fee. Secondary Skills: Any. Weapon Proficiencies: Any. Nonweapon Proficiencies: Required: None. Recommended: Player’s choice; among those that may be selected are Alertness, Boating, Fast-talking, Infor- mation Gathering, and Looting. Skill Progression: Adventurer thieves tend to spread their skill im- provements as evenly as possible, to al- low them to deal with the many different challenges the adventuring life presents. If there is any concentration, it i

Taste is a fairly reliable, if dangerous, ethod of identifying a poison. It car- s a -5% penalty. After dabbing a ny bit on his tongue, the Assassin spits out. There is still a chance that the oison will affect the Assassin: 25% for jected poison, 75% for ingested, and 0% for contact. The poison's effects, any, are half strength (see above). The most certain way of identifying a son is by its symptom (no penalty on attempt). The drawback of this thod is of course that you need a poi- ned character to examine. n Assassin with herbalism prof
[PDF_PAGE 100 | score=4] Wax Pad This is a small dock of wax usually kept in a rigid :ontainer to keep it in one piece-the quivalent of a large snuff-box will do qicely. The thief uses the wax to record m impression of a key he wants to have iuplicated later. Obviously, the thief :an only do this if he has access to a tey, at least for a short time, and if he doesn’t wish to take a chance on bor- rowing the key for a while. From the rvax impression, the thief can have a lbcksmith make a duplicate of the origi- tal key, or attempt to make one himself ising a keymaking set, if he has one. A backstab is not always liter- lly a stab in the back!
